water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: Light and pressure do not strongly push the outlook either way.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing is very good on the Chattahoochee River with ideal water temperature and recent stocking supporting multiple fly options. Expect trout to feed comfortably near cover in runs, riffles, and seams with light sky cover and moderate wind.
generated_text.best_window: The best window is day1 morning because water temperature is ideal, supporting active trout in riffles, runs, seams, and structure, plus recent stocking enhances bite confidence using eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 57.9°F, flow is 933 cfs, gage height is 2.01 ft, and turbidity is 6 NTU at CHATTAHOOCHEE RIVER NEAR NORCROSS, GA.
generated_text.weather_setup: Sky cover averages 20.79%, pressure is 30.05 inHg with neutral trend, wind averages 5.3 mph with gusts up to 15 mph, and rain probability is low with no expected rain.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout are likely positioned in comfortable feeding lanes near riffles, runs, seams, and structure. Present clean drifts with smaller natural nymphs, midges, soft hackles, and opportunistic streamers, incorporating stocked-trout attractors like egg patterns and squirmy worms. Fish cautiously during higher light periods to avoid spooking stocked trout.
